posts.subhook: Modern Science · Regional Recipes · From the Sea
posts.caption: Littoralicious — a journal of modern food science and regional cooking, written from the water's edge. For yacht chefs who put up a hundred covers on a moving boat, and for curious cooks who want to know why food behaves the way it does. posts.hook: Where the science of cooking meets the flavour of a coastline
posts.subhook: Not science for its own sake. Not nostalgia for a place. The point where the two meet on the plate.
posts.caption: That's the whole idea behind Littoralicious. We take the questions a chef actually asks at the pass — why did the sauce split, why won't the white set, how do I feed fifteen at anchor — and answer them with tested method and a cited source. Then we root that method in a real coastline: the produce, the dishes, the way a place cooks. Rigorous, tested, and rooted in place. A journal for yacht chefs and curious cooks. littoralicious.com — link in bio.
posts.fact: Littoralicious answers the questions a chef asks at the pass with tested, cited method — then roots that method in the coastline the dish comes from.

posts.subhook: Modern science, regional recipes, and cooking from the sea.

Regional recipes: the dishes of a coastline, cooked with respect for where they come from. From the sea: provisioned, plated, and served at the water's edge. posts.fact: Littoralicious rests on three pillars — modern science, regional recipes, and cooking from the sea — each built for chefs working at the water's edge.

posts.subhook: Custard yolk at 63 °C, but the white needs 80 °C.
posts.caption: The yolk sets before the bulk of the white does, and that single inversion decides how you poach at volume. More than half the egg white — about 54% — is ovalbumin, and it does not firm until around 80 °C (176 °F). The yolk, meanwhile, turns to custard by about 63 °C (145 °F) and only sets firm near 70 °C (158 °F). Those bands sit roughly 17 °C apart, so no steady bath gives a firm free-form white and a runny yolk at the same time. Hold eggs in a 63 °C onsen bath and you get a glossy custard yolk while half the white never coagulates: perfect in a rice bowl or ramen, a watery puddle under a poached egg on toast. The lesson for a busy pass is to stop chasing one magic temperature. Decide which texture the dish wants first — firm free-form white or soft custard yolk — and let that choose the method. The protein does not negotiate; your service plan has to bend. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: The yolk turns to custard around 63 °C while the bulk of the white (54% ovalbumin) does not firm until about 80 °C, so no single bath gives both a firm white and a runny yolk.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil cross-section of an egg on cool paper stock, the custard yolk core and surrounding white labelled with two temperature zones, 63 °C and 80 °C, graphite hatching.
posts.why: Leads with the counterintuitive chemistry that reframes every poaching problem — a save-and-share fact that positions the piece as the definitive explainer.

posts.subhook: One cook, a hundred covers, not one poached to order.
posts.caption: A single cook can put up a hundred poached eggs on a moving boat because the eggs are never poached to order — they are cooked ahead and only reheated on the pass. The line has five moves. One: strain each egg through a fine-mesh sieve to drain the loose, watery white that would otherwise feather. Two: par-poach batches of six to eight in a bare, smiling simmer at 82 °C (180 °F) for about two to two and a half minutes, roughly 30 seconds under done. Three: ice-shock at once to stop the cook and firm them for handling. Four: hold cold, fully submerged in fresh water at or below 5 °C (41 °F), for up to three days. Five: fire to order in a perforated tray lowered into 66 °C (150 °F) water for three minutes — it holds there about 20 minutes without overcooking, and you lift the whole tray in one motion to drain. Now the egg is no longer the bottleneck; assembly is. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A make-ahead poach — strain, par-poach 30 seconds short at 82 °C, ice-shock, cold-hold up to three days at 5 °C, then reheat to order in 66 °C water — lets one cook plate a firm poached white across a hundred covers without poaching to order.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil five-panel strip, one numbered panel per step (fine-mesh strainer, smiling simmer, ice bath, cold-hold tub, perforated tray in warm water), graphite on cool paper, one panel per carousel slide.
posts.why: The centrepiece workflow as a step carousel — the highest-utility save for anyone running brunch, and the parameters drive full-article reads.

posts.subhook: The 3.5-minute figure is for liquid egg — it will not pasteurise a shell.
posts.caption: In-shell pasteurisation of a whole egg is about 57 °C (135 °F) held for roughly 75 minutes — not the 60 °C for 3.5 minutes figure that gets repeated in kitchens. That shorter number is the standard for liquid egg out of the shell; three and a half minutes cannot drive heat through an intact shell and reach the yolk, so using it on whole eggs leaves the centre under-treated. The two specifications get conflated constantly, and this is one of the few egg errors that is a genuine safety risk rather than a matter of texture. If you want a guaranteed-safe runny yolk, hold the shell egg at about 57 °C for about 75 minutes: long enough for heat to penetrate to the centre while keeping the white liquid and workable. Good news for anyone running an onsen bath — a 63 °C (145 °F) hold for 45 to 60 minutes clears the food-safety target with margin, so that gentle cook doubles as a kill step. Use clean, well-refrigerated eggs and serve hot. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Whole-shell egg pasteurisation is about 57 °C for about 75 minutes; the 60 °C for 3.5 minutes figure is for liquid egg and cannot pasteurise through an intact shell.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate of a whole egg in a water bath, thermometer reading 57 °C and a 75 min annotation, a faded 60 °C / 3.5 min label struck through, graphite on cool paper.
posts.why: A genuine safety correction travels far and builds trust; the myth-bust format earns shares among working chefs.

posts.subhook: White rice to steel-cut oats — one dial that never moves.
posts.caption: A steam oven runs at one setpoint for everything: 100% steam, 100°C (212°F). It never changes. What changes is only the water ratio and the minutes on the clock — two honest numbers instead of a heat dial you keep second-guessing. White long-grain: 1 part grain to 1.5 water, 18–23 minutes. Basmati: 1:1.5, about 15 minutes, the fastest white. Short-grain sushi: 1:1.2 by weight, roughly 30 minutes. Quinoa: 1:1.5, 25 minutes. Coarse polenta: 1:3, 45 minutes, no stirring and no scorching. Steel-cut oats: 1:3–4, around an hour. Same dial, every line. The physics is why: saturated steam is pinned to 100°C at sea level, comfortably above the point at which any cereal's starch gelatinises (rice finishes near 76–78°C), so heat is never the limiting factor — only water and time are. Treat every figure as a calibrated starting point, not gospel: grain age, variety and your oven all shift them. Run a tray, taste, note the number that worked, and laminate your own card. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: In a steam oven every grain cooks at one fixed setpoint — 100% steam, 100°C — and you change only the water ratio and the minutes.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: an oven dial locked at 100°C above a fanned row of numbered grain swatches (white rice through steel-cut oats), graphite on cool paper — slide 1 cover, later slides render the grain table rows.
posts.why: The saveable reference card — a carousel built around a real lookup table drives bookmarks and shares among working chefs.

posts.hook: Read the starch, not the shape
posts.subhook: Why long-grain jasmine still cooks soft and clingy.
posts.caption: Two grains of identical length can behave completely differently in the pan, and the reason is a molecule, not a shape. Starch is two polymers: linear amylose and branched amylopectin. High-amylose grains gelatinise higher, drink more water and swell into firm, dry, separate grains; high-amylopectin (waxy) grains cook lower, softer and stickier. Sushi rice is low-amylose (about 16–19%) and cooks tender and cohesive; long-grain and basmati are intermediate (about 20–23%) and cook separate. The tell that shape is a decoy: Thai jasmine is long-grained yet cooks soft and clingy, because its amylose is variable and often lower than basmati's — roughly 15–22% depending on cultivar. So read the starch, not the silhouette. It also explains the water column in any grain table: as amylose climbs, the grain wants more water and a longer clock. Once you know a grain's amylose, its texture and its ratio stop being a guess and start being predictable. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Amylose content, not grain length, decides whether cooked grains turn out sticky or separate — which is why long-grain Thai jasmine still cooks soft and clingy.
posts.brief: B&W macro of three grains in one frame under raking light — sushi, basmati, jasmine — with a hand-drawn amylose spiral overlaid as pencil annotation.
posts.why: A crisp myth-correction that signals expertise and rewards the scroll with a fact chefs can use tomorrow.

posts.subhook: Bacillus cereus survives the pot — cool fast or don't reheat.
posts.caption: Uncooked rice carries Bacillus cereus spores, and they survive cooking — boiling or steaming does not kill them. Leave cooked rice sitting in the 10–60°C (50–140°F) danger zone and those spores germinate and produce cereulide, a toxin that is heat-stable. Reheating destroys the bacteria but not the toxin, which is why a hot pass cannot rescue rice that sat warm too long. On a boat cooking rice in bulk, this is the failure that ends careers, not just service. The control is speed and cold. Par-cook, spread the rice thin (25 mm or less) on trays to stop carry-over and speed cooling, then blast-chill: from 57°C to 21°C (135 to 70°F) within two hours, and on to 5°C (41°F) or below — UK guidance says start chilling within about 90 minutes. Hold at 5°C or colder for no more than a day, then regenerate once, with steam, to a 74°C (165°F) core. Never warm-hold cooked rice. Cool it fast, hold it cold, reheat it once. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Bacillus cereus spores survive cooking and, if rice is held warm, make the heat-stable toxin cereulide that reheating cannot destroy — so cool fast, hold cold, reheat once to 74°C.
posts.brief: Stark B&W: a lidded pan of rice on a warm pass with condensation on the lid, a pencil-drawn thermometer plunging through a shaded 10–60°C danger-zone band.
posts.why: Career-critical food-safety fact with real urgency — the kind of post a chef forwards to their whole crew.

taking-a-shot.title: Taking a Hard Shot Without Flinching

posts.hook: The burn is your own heat
posts.subhook: The alarm quietly reset to 34°C — below your own mouth.
posts.caption: A neat spirit's burn is not a flavour and not really heat — it is a false alarm. The channel behind it, TRPV1, is the same one chilli fires, and it normally opens only at genuinely dangerous heat, around 43°C (109°F). Ethanol sabotages that setting: in the founding experiment it dropped the channel's trigger to about 34°C (93°F). Your mouth sits at roughly 35-37°C (95-99°F). So the maths is brutal — once ethanol has reset the alarm, your own body heat is already above the trigger, and the burn fires with nothing hot in the glass at all. That single number governs the whole technique. You are not numbing anything; you are denying the alarm a threshold to cross. Keep the tissue and the liquid below 34°C and the channel has nothing to fire on. The receptor science behind this is Grade A, replicated across decades — the direction is solid even where the ritual around it is not. Knowing why it burns is what turns flinching from willpower into a procedure. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Ethanol lowers the mouth's TRPV1 heat alarm from about 42°C to about 34°C (93°F), below your own 35-37°C mouth, so the 'burn' is your own body heat tripping a sabotaged trigger rather than the spirit being hot.
posts.brief: B&W technical-pencil plate: a mouth cross-section over a full shot glass, a dial arrow dropping from 42°C to 34°C, graphite annotation reading 'your own heat', hard side light, no colour.
posts.why: Leads with the counterintuitive core fact — your own heat, not the spirit — the single idea that makes a reader need the full method.

posts.subhook: Everyone freezes the bottle. Nobody cools the mouth.
posts.caption: Everyone freezes the bottle. Almost nobody chills the surface the spirit actually lands on. Hold a sip of ice water or a small ice chip in your mouth for about three seconds, swallow, and you drop your oral tissue below the 34°C (93°F) trigger that ethanol has lowered the heat alarm to. Now the drink meets a mouth that is already too cold for the channel to fire. Cold is the only lever that genuinely touches the burn, because the channel is temperature-gated: cooling tissue toward 25°C (77°F) can abolish a comparable capsaicin burn outright, while warming the same tissue intensifies it. Serve the spirit straight from the freezer at -18°C (0°F); a 40%-ABV spirit will not solidify, since it freezes near -27°C (-17°F), so the freezer is both safe storage and the best serve. Cold does two jobs at once — it quiets the channel and collapses ethanol's vapour pressure, so fewer harsh fumes reach your nose. Freezing the bottle is the step everyone knows; pre-cooling your own mouth is the free one nobody uses. Mise en place for your palate. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Holding ice water or a chip in your mouth for about three seconds before the shot drops your oral tissue below the 34°C (93°F) trigger, so cooling your mouth — not just freezing the bottle — is what actually switches the burn off.
posts.brief: B&W slow-motion reel: an ice chip held at the lips with a 3-count on screen, then a freezer-frosted pour and one fast swallow — hard side light, condensation beading, graphite tones, no colour.
posts.why: A visible, do-it-tonight technique; the 'chill your mouth, not the bottle' move is the ownable, shareable action best carried by motion.

posts.subhook: Casein grabs oil. Ethanol is not oil.
posts.caption: Reach for milk after a hard shot and you are running the wrong playbook. Milk really does put out a chilli fire — its casein protein strips the oil-soluble capsaicin off your receptors. But ethanol is water-soluble, so there is nothing for the casein to grab. The analogy simply fails. The fizzy chaser is worse: carbonation is its own irritant, because dissolved CO2 is sensed as a sting, so a cola or soda adds bite at the exact moment you are trying to remove it. What actually works stacks three real mechanisms — cold lowers the channel's firing, dilution rinses ethanol off the tissue, and sweetness suppresses chemical irritation across the senses. So the chaser that earns its place is cold, sweet and still, or simply cold water. One honest caveat for the galley: ignore precise folklore like 'cold cuts irritation 38 per cent' — the direction is real, but the decimals are invented marketing. Build the chaser on the mechanism, not on the myth you inherited behind the bar. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Milk quells a chilli fire because casein strips oil-soluble capsaicin, but ethanol is water-soluble so milk does nothing, and fizzy chasers add sting because dissolved CO2 is itself an irritant — the working chaser is cold, sweet and still.
posts.brief: B&W still life: a glass of milk and a fizzing soda struck through in pencil beside a plain glass of still water, technical-pencil labels reading 'oil-soluble' vs 'water-soluble', graphite tones, no colour.
posts.why: Myth-bust with immediate utility at any bar; corrects two chasers everyone gets wrong, high save-and-share value.

hash-browns.title: Oven Hash Browns: The Sheet-Pan Slab

posts.hook: The fix is never more fat
posts.subhook: Squeeze the grated potato 30% lighter — crisp is built by dryness, not oil.
posts.caption: Grate 2 kg of potato, salt it with 12 g of fine sea salt, rest 10 minutes, then squeeze it in a towel until it weighs roughly 30% less than it did grated. That number is the whole dish. Water left in the shred flashes to steam in the oven, and steam is the opposite of crisp — which is why most hash browns come out pale, gummy and oil-logged no matter how much fat goes in. The fix is never more fat; it is less water. Salt does the first shift, pulling moisture out by osmosis while the potato stands; the towel does the rest. Weigh before and after if you are unsure — a 2 kg grate should press down to about 1.4 kg. From there the crust builds itself: a sheet pan preheated in a 230°C oven sears the base by direct conduction like a flat-top, and one slab feeds 10–12 crew with barely a hand laid on it. The rösti farmers of Bern and the diner cooks of New York never argued the recipe. They argued the squeeze. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Hash-brown crisp is built by dryness, not fat — salt with 12 g per 2 kg, rest 10 minutes, then squeeze the grated potato about 30% lighter, because leftover water flashes to steam and steam kills the crust.
posts.brief: B&W photo: two hands wringing a towel-wrapped bundle of grated potato over a steel bowl, water streaming out in hard backlight — grain and texture forward.
posts.why: The article's core ownable fact, with a weighable number any chef can test at tomorrow's breakfast — posted when breakfast is on their mind.

posts.hook: Rinse starch off, dust starch on
posts.subhook: Two starches, opposite jobs — one a gummy paste, one a glass shell.
posts.caption: The same molecule ruins one hash brown and rescues the next. Loose surface starch, freed when you grate, gelatinises into a gluey paste — so you rinse the shreds in cold water until it runs clear and send it down the drain. Then you add starch straight back: a thin coat of cornstarch at 2% of the squeezed potato's weight. Heated past about 65–70°C it gelatinises too, but spread as a film over dry shreds it dehydrates in the oven into a glassy, brittle shell that fractures under the knife. Same chemistry, opposite jobs — one starch you remove, one you recruit. The rest of the coat is 4% fat, tossed through until every shred is glossed, carrying heat to the surface and driving the browning. Both percentages are measured against the squeezed weight, not the grated weight, which is why the squeeze comes first and the maths come after. Rinsing starch off a dish that lives on starch feels wrong the first time you do it. It is the wrong starch you are losing. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Rinse off the loose surface starch that turns gummy, then coat with 2% cornstarch of the squeezed weight — it gelatinises past 65–70°C and dries in the oven into a glassy, shatter-crisp shell.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: a single potato shred in cross-section with the thin cornstarch film labelled as the glass shell — paired arrows showing loose starch rinsed away vs the coat recruited, food-science diagram style.
posts.why: The paradox (remove starch, add starch) stops the scroll and flatters the reader's craft — pure science-pillar content for the midday feed.

posts.hook: The crust dies under a lid
posts.subhook: Par-bake pale, crisp to order — the charter split-cook.
posts.caption: Hash browns punish holding — cover a crisp slab and the trapped steam softens the crust within minutes. So the charter move is to split the cook. Par-bake the slab to pale gold, about 15 minutes at 230°C, and cool it. At service, finish-crisp it 8–10 minutes in the same 230°C oven and it plates in minutes across a staggered crew breakfast. Cooled and wrapped well, the par-baked slab freezes for up to 1 month and crisps straight from frozen, no thaw. One half-sheet feeds 10–12; for bigger numbers, run two trays on staggered timings rather than one thick, slow slab. At sea the firm-pressed slab earns its keep twice over: it will not slide or scatter with the swell the way loose patties do, and it cuts to order straight off the tray. The one unbreakable rule sits at the end of the prep sheet: never cover, stack or hold it hot — steam undoes in three minutes what the oven spent half an hour building. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Split the cook for charter service: par-bake the slab pale (~15 min at 230°C), finish-crisp 8–10 min to order — it freezes up to 1 month and crisps from frozen, but covering or stacking it hot steams the crust soft in minutes.
posts.brief: B&W overhead photo for the cover: the pale par-baked slab on a half-sheet beside its deep-gold finished twin, knife mid-cut into squares — the before/after in one frame; inner slides as white cards, one prep rule each.
posts.why: The charter prep table genuinely carries a slide per rule (par-bake, freeze, scale, never cover) — save-and-share utility for chefs planning the next service.

posts.subhook: Salamoia, steam, and the Ligurian crust
posts.caption: The dimples in a Ligurian focaccia are not decoration — they are steam pockets waiting to happen. Genoa floods them with salamoia: 4 g of fine sea salt whisked into 30 g of warm water, then 40 g of olive oil whisked in until it turns cloudy. Spoon it over the proofed dough, press your fingers to the pan, and bake at 230°C. In the oven the water in each crater flashes to steam and lifts the dough around it; the salt has been carried into the crumb, and the oil left behind fries the surface crisp. That is the whole trick most recipes miss: oil crisps and flavours the crust, but it does not make the holes. Water does — 80% hydration inside the dough for the open, custardy crumb, and the brine on top for the crater rims. If your focaccia comes out greasy and pale, the fix was never more oil. It was salt water. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: The salamoia brine (4 g salt, 30 g warm water, 40 g oil) flashes to steam in the dimples — water, not oil, creates focaccia's lift and open crumb; oil only crisps and flavours the crust.
posts.brief: B&W macro, hard side light: oiled fingertips pressing into slack glossy dough, cloudy salamoia pooling in the craters — the sheen carries the frame.
posts.why: Myth-bust on the article's one ownable idea, and the dimple-and-brine move is the most filmable motion in the piece.

posts.subhook: 24–72 hours at 4°C, enzymes on the night shift
posts.caption: At 4°C, baker's yeast all but stops — but the enzymes working alongside it do not. Park a focaccia dough in the fridge for 24 to 72 hours and amylases keep carving starch into sugars, proteases loosen the gluten, and slow fermentation lays down organic acids. You taste the result as a nutty, faintly sour depth no same-day dough can fake, and you feel it as a dough that stretches to the corners of the tin where a young one would tear back. The recipe runs low yeast on purpose — 0.6% of the flour weight, 3 g in a 500 g mix — so the cold days, not the yeast, carry the flavour. 48 hours is the sweet spot; past 72 the yeast outruns the flavour and the dough turns boozy. The active work in the whole recipe is 25 minutes. The rest is a fridge shelf doing more for the crumb than your hands ever could. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: At 4°C yeast nearly stops but amylases and proteases keep working, so a 24–72 h cold ferment (48 h sweet spot, 0.6% yeast) builds flavour and extensibility no same-day dough can match.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: a dough tub in cross-section at 4°C, graphite call-outs of amylase and protease carving starch chains — near-black linework on cool paper.
posts.why: Counter-intuitive patience story that positions the science voice and saves chefs a failed same-day bake — weekend bakers plan their ferment here.

posts.subhook: Charter prep: the cold ferment works while you do
posts.caption: One focaccia dough, mixed on provisioning day, holds three days at 4°C — which means you bake to order across a whole charter leg without a fresh mix. The cold ferment is the make-ahead: it builds flavour while it waits, so day-three bread is better than day-one, not worse. The service maths: par-bake trays to pale at about 16 minutes, cool them, then finish 5 to 6 minutes at 230°C when the table is seated — hot focaccia on demand. After the folds the dough also freezes raw in its tub; thaw overnight and it ferments as it goes. Baked and cooled, it freezes for a month and refreshes in 8 to 10 minutes at 180°C from frozen. Two rules guard the crust: never the microwave, which steams it soft, and never the fridge for baked bread — it stales fastest at exactly fridge temperature. One 23 × 33 cm tray feeds 10 to 12; double into a half gastronorm for crew and guests. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Focaccia dough mixed on provisioning day holds 3 days at 4°C and par-bakes (16 min pale, finished 5–6 min at 230°C at service) — bread to order across a charter leg from one mix.
posts.brief: B&W flat-lay in galley steel: three chalk-labelled dough tubs (day 1 / 2 / 3) beside a half-gastronorm tray, overhead light, no styling.
posts.why: Pure galley utility — the charter prep and storage table maps one-to-one onto slides (make-ahead, par-bake, freeze raw, freeze baked, refresh) and lands mid-week when chefs plan provisioning.

quiche.title: The Crew Quiche: The Science-First Version

posts.hook: One litre, eight eggs, two yolks
posts.subhook: The whole recipe is one ratio — the rest is garnish.
posts.caption: One litre of dairy, eight whole eggs, two extra yolks — that is the entire quiche. Everything else is garnish. Split the dairy 60% milk to 40% cream, salt at roughly 1%, and the appareil sets silky yet sliceable at any scale: one tart tin, a half gastronorm feeding 10–12 crew, or a full GN for a combined crew-and-guest lunch. The ratio is also a dial. Drop to 6 eggs per litre and the custard softens enough to plate as a starter; push to 12 eggs per litre and you get a firm quiche that survives a passage in a lunchbox without weeping. The extra yolks are not optional — yolk lecithin and fat set lower and softer than white, which is what keeps the slice tender instead of rubbery. Weigh it rather than eyeballing: 800 g milk, 600 g cream, 12 eggs and 3 yolks fills a half gastronorm. Once the number lives in your head, quiche stops being a recipe you look up and becomes a container you fill — bacon, smoked salmon, leeks, whatever the fridge owes you. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A quiche appareil is a ratio, not a recipe: 1 L dairy (60% milk / 40% cream) to 8 whole eggs + 2 yolks at ~1% salt, dialled down to 6 eggs/L for a softer set or up to 12 eggs/L for a slice that survives a passage.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: a labelled ratio diagram — litre jug, eight eggs, two yolks — graphite line-work with the 60/40 dairy split annotated like an engineering drawing.
posts.why: A memorisable ratio card is peak save-and-share utility for working chefs planning the next crew lunch.

posts.subhook: Why custard weeps, and the temperature that stops it.
posts.caption: A weeping quiche did not leak — it seized. Egg proteins start knitting into a gel at about 62°C and lock up by 85°C; pushed past that, the network over-tightens and squeezes its own water back out. That is syneresis, the same failure that turns scrambled eggs grainy, and no amount of draining the bacon will fix it, because the water is coming from the custard itself. The cure is temperature discipline at both ends. Bake at 150°C, not 180°C, so the set creeps up gently through the pan. Then pull it when the edges are set but the centre still jiggles like soft jelly — 73–75°C on a probe. It looks underdone. It is not: a 20–30 minute rest carries the centre to roughly 80°C and firms it for a clean 2×5 cut. Chase the number, not the clock — a half gastronorm takes 45–55 minutes, but ovens lie, and a galley oven on a moving boat lies more than most. Trust the probe and the jiggle, and the slice holds its shape across a whole watch. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Egg proteins gel from ~62°C and squeeze out their own water when overheated (syneresis), so a quiche bakes at 150°C and comes out at 73–75°C internal — still jiggling — because a 20–30 minute rest carries it to ~80°C.
posts.brief: B&W reel frames: the pan nudged and the centre wobbling like soft jelly in slow motion, then a probe thermometer reading 74°C in tight close-up, high-contrast grain.
posts.why: The jiggle test is watch-and-learn motion — a reel carries the one visible proof of the science to non-followers in the evening scroll.

posts.hook: Brush the hot shell with egg
posts.subhook: The two-minute waterproof film under every clean slice.
posts.caption: Brush the hot, blind-baked shell with beaten egg and give it 2 minutes back in the oven until it dries to a sheen. That thin coagulated film is waterproof — the single fix for the soggy bottom, the step professional kitchens swear by and home cooks skip. The logic is blunt: a raw or unsealed base never recovers once a litre of wet custard lands on it. So the shell earns three defences before the appareil is poured. First, a full blind bake — 180°C with beans for 20 minutes, then 8–10 minutes uncovered until dry and pale gold. Second, the egg wash, set to that dry sheen. Third, half the grated Gruyère scattered straight onto the sealed base — a fatty raft between custard and pastry. Do all three and the base stays crisp through the 150°C bake, through the rest, and through 3 days in the fridge — which is what lets one half gastronorm feed a crew across an entire watch of staggered meal times without a reheat. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Egg-washing the hot blind-baked shell and setting it for 2 minutes forms a waterproof coagulated film — the fix for the soggy bottom, reinforced by a layer of cheese laid directly on the sealed base.
posts.brief: B&W overhead carousel: pastry brush glazing the pale-gold shell with egg wash catching the light as a sheen, then the cheese raft going down, then the clean-based slice in cross-section.
posts.why: Three sequential seals from the article's method genuinely carry three slides, landing in the weekend-brunch quiche mindset.

posts.hook: The pocket is steam, not yeast
posts.subhook: The balloon is a race between shell and steam
posts.caption: Yeast never lifts a pita. It builds the dough — the pocket itself is water flashing to steam against a surface so hot that the two outer skins set before the gas can leak. Above roughly 230°C, the round's surface dries into a thin shell of ungelatinised starch: a gas-tight barrier. Inside, pressure climbs until the dough splits cleanly along its middle and the two skins blow apart into one balloon. On a 275–290°C stone that takes 2–3 minutes; on a dry skillet at maximum heat, about 30 seconds on the first side, then a flip, then the balloon in 1–2 minutes. Lose the race and the failure is legible: too cool and the steam seeps out before the shell sets; too thick and the skins never separate. Which means a flat pita is almost never the recipe's fault — it is a heat failure or a rolling failure, and both are fixable. Preheat until a drop of water skitters and vanishes instantly, keep the round an even 4–5 mm, and the balloon is inevitable. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: The pita pocket is leavened by steam, not yeast — the two outer skins must set above ~230°C before the gas can leak, or the bread stays flat.
posts.brief: Locked-off overhead B&W reel: the round hits the dry cast-iron skillet, 30 s, flip, then the balloon inflating in real time — grain-heavy, single take, no cuts.
posts.why: The ballooning shot is the natural thumb-stopper; the physics turns the spectacle into a lesson worth following.

posts.subhook: Flour 100 · water 64 · salt 2 — the rest is heat
posts.caption: 64% hydration is the whole pita recipe. Flour at 100%, water 64%, olive oil 3%, salt 2%, sugar 1.5%, yeast 0.8% — everything else is heat and an even round. The window is narrow and physical: below 60% water the dough cannot generate the steam that inflates the pocket; above roughly 68% it turns too slack to roll to the even 4–5 mm the balloon demands. 64% is the hand-rolled sweet spot — enough water to blow the two skins apart, enough structure to survive the pin. And because it is a ratio, it scales without arithmetic drama: 1,000 g of bread flour and 640 g of lukewarm water at 28–30°C gives twenty 85 g pitas, enough for ten to twelve at the table; halve it for crew lunch, double it for a charter's worth. One refinement carries real weight: add the 20 g of salt only after flour and water have hydrated, so it tightens the gluten network without fighting the flour for water. Learn the number once and every pita after is a multiplication. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Pita's hydration window is narrow — below 60% water the pocket starves for steam, above ~68% the dough is too slack to roll; 64% is the hand-rolled sweet spot.
posts.brief: B&W flat-lay photo: a scale with a mound of flour and a beaker of water beside it, the baker's-percentage ratio hand-lettered in graphite beneath.
posts.why: Ratios get saved — a screenshot-able number that rescales makes the post a reference card, not a scroll-past.

posts.subhook: What a 14,400-year-old flatbread teaches the galley
posts.caption: The oldest bread ever found predates agriculture by more than 4,000 years. In 2018, archaeologists at Shubayqa 1 in north-eastern Jordan dated charred flatbread crumbs from a Natufian hearth to 14,400 years ago — ground wild cereals, baked on hot stone beside a fire, no oven in sight. That detail is the working lesson: pita was a hearth bread for millennia before it was an oven bread. The surface, not the cavity, does the cooking. Which is why, at sea, a bare cast-iron skillet at maximum heat out-bakes a struggling domestic oven: it needs no preheated cavity, holds a ~285°C surface through round after round and through swell, and turns out one fresh pocket at a time exactly when service calls for it. Pair it with the make-ahead — halve the yeast and cold-ferment the dough up to 72 h at 4°C — and one provisioning-day mix becomes hot pita to order across a whole charter leg. Fourteen millennia of technique, and the galley route is still the original one. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Pita was a hearth bread — the 14,400-year-old original was baked on hot stone with no oven — so a bare cast-iron skillet at maximum heat out-bakes a domestic oven at sea.
posts.brief: B&W photo: a single pita ballooning on a dark cast-iron skillet over a galley hob, hard side light, deep charcoal shadows.
posts.why: Heritage plus a no-oven galley payoff is the ownable yacht-chef angle a generic food page cannot copy.

carrot-cake.title: Moist Carrot Cake: The Science-First Version

posts.subhook: Twenty minutes of osmosis before the oven sees anything
posts.caption: Toss 400 g of grated carrot with 30 g of brown sugar and 3 g of salt, wait 20 minutes, and it surrenders roughly 30% of its weight in water. That is osmosis doing work no oven can: dissolved solutes outside the cell walls pull the free water through, the same mechanism as a quick pickle. Squeeze the shreds hard in a clean towel until the pile weighs 270–280 g and feels almost dry to the touch. Every gram you press out was headed straight for your batter — the reason most carrot cake bakes up gummy by day one. The sugars, carotenoids and aromatics stay behind, concentrated, so the crumb reads genuinely moist for three days instead. Reserve the expelled liquid: reduced with 20 g of sugar it becomes an amber glaze to brush over the warm cake, a flavour echo from what you removed. Nothing on the ingredients list changes. The cake is decided at the colander, twenty minutes before the oven is even on. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Macerating grated carrot with salt and sugar for 20 minutes, then pressing it hard, removes roughly 30% of its weight in water — concentrating flavour instead of flooding the batter.
posts.brief: B&W overhead reel key-frame: hands wringing grated carrot in a linen towel over a steel bowl, water mid-stream, hard side light on the twist.
posts.why: The squeeze is the article's proof in visible motion — water physically leaving the carrot makes the invisible step undeniable in a 15-second reel.

posts.subhook: Blitz the roast into the fat phase, not the odd bite
posts.caption: A whole walnut piece only gives up its roast when someone happens to bite it. Blitzed into the fat phase, every forkful carries it. Toast 160 g of walnut halves at 165°C for 10–12 minutes, until the galley smells of patisserie, then split them: 80 g stay whole for the top, 80 g go into the processor for 3–4 minutes until they collapse into a smooth, pourable butter. Whisk 60 g of that butter into the wet mix. Toasting drives the Maillard and Strecker reactions that build pyrazines and 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline — the compound behind popcorn and basmati rice. Locked inside whole pieces, those volatiles sit stranded in the odd lucky bite; dispersed through the oil, they season the entire crumb. It is the difference between a cake with walnuts in it and a walnut cake. The nut weight on the ingredient list barely changes — only where the flavour lives does. The same move works on any nut bake you run this season. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Blitzing toasted walnuts into a pourable butter disperses their Maillard aromatics through the batter's fat phase, so every bite carries the roast instead of stranding it in occasional whole pieces.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: a walnut half in cross-section beside a spoonful of pourable walnut butter, aroma lines rising, graphite on cool paper.
posts.why: A counter-intuitive craft rule a chef can apply to any nut bake tonight — high save value on a quick break-time read.

posts.subhook: Cold, brief, and cut with mascarpone — built for a 28°C galley
posts.caption: Cream cheese frosting fails warm because of water, not sugar. Cream cheese runs only about 33% fat; beaten soft and warm it traps air, then collapses and weeps the moment plates leave the fridge. The fix is twofold. First, temperature and restraint: paddle the cheese cold, straight from the fridge, for 30 seconds only — incorporate, never whip. Second, composition: replace part of it with mascarpone at 60–75% fat, lifting the fat-to-water ratio of the whole emulsion. The build in this Blueprint — 340 g cold cream cheese, 115 g cold mascarpone, 85 g soft butter, 280 g sifted icing sugar — holds a clean edge to 28°C without breaking. The history is worth knowing too: the pairing everyone treats as carrot cake's birthright only set in 1965, when Kraft printed a cream-cheese-iced carrot cake for Philadelphia. The cake is medieval; its signature finish is younger than the food processor. Built cold and fat, it finally deserves the job. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Keeping cream cheese cold, mixing it briefly instead of whipping, and cutting it with 60–75%-fat mascarpone lifts the frosting's fat-to-water ratio so it holds at 28°C without weeping.
posts.brief: B&W macro: an offset palette knife pulling one clean 1 cm ridge of frosting across a sheet cake, cold sheen, deep shadow.
posts.why: Solves the warm-service frosting failure every charter chef has lived through — lands on bake-day planning and invites war stories in the comments.

sticky-toffee-pudding.title: Sticky Toffee Pudding: Dark Muscovado & Whisky Caramel

posts.hook: Sticky toffee is not a cake
posts.subhook: Six grams of baking soda build a sponge that drinks
posts.caption: Stir 6 g of baking soda into 280 g of boiling water before it touches the dates, and the soak's pH climbs from about 5 to about 8. In that alkaline water the dates' pectin and cell walls break down fast: the puree blends silky instead of stringy, and the soda spends the fruit's native acid early so it never fights the baking powder later. The result is deliberate — a low, tight, porous sponge, not a tall fluffy cake. That matters because sticky toffee pudding is not really a cake at all. It is a sponge engineered to drink: skewer it warm, pour hot sauce over, and the crumb should pull the toffee down until the surface goes dark and glossy. A springy, over-aerated sponge sheds sauce; this one holds roughly 40 g a portion. One warning from the galley: cool the puree to 40°C or below before it meets the creamed butter and sugar, or it melts the emulsion and the crumb turns gummy. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Stirring 6 g of baking soda into the boiling date soak raises its pH from about 5 to about 8, dissolving the dates' pectin so the puree turns silky and the sponge stays low and porous enough to drink hot sauce.
posts.brief: B&W technical-pencil plate: cross-section of the skewered sponge mid-pour, graphite arrows tracing the sauce wicking down into the crumb.
posts.why: The counterintuitive soda-in-the-soak chemistry is the article's ownable move — it stops the scroll of anyone who has ever baked a stringy date sponge.

posts.subhook: The sugar decision that separates caramel from burnt sugar and dark fruit
posts.caption: Dark muscovado carries up to about 15% molasses by weight. Light brown sugar carries roughly 3.5%. That gap is the whole difference between a pudding that tastes of caramel and one that tastes of burnt sugar and dark fruit — the smoke, the liquorice edge, the colour that reads toffee before the sauce even arrives. The molasses brings invert sugars too, which hold moisture in the crumb for days: useful when dessert is baked on Tuesday for a Thursday charter dinner. This recipe runs 175 g of muscovado plus 25 g of black treacle in the sponge and another 150 g in the whisky caramel — the same sugar top and bottom, so sauce and crumb speak with one voice. If the boat's dry store only has light brown, add 25 g of molasses to compensate; without it the pudding tastes flat no matter how good the dates are. Cream the muscovado with 100 g of butter for a full 3–4 minutes; a little residual grain is fine. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Dark muscovado carries up to about 15% molasses by weight against light brown sugar's roughly 3.5%, bringing deeper colour, smoke and invert sugars that keep the crumb moist for days.
posts.brief: B&W macro photo: two sugar mounds side by side — muscovado's wet clumps against light brown's dry sand — raking side light for texture.
posts.why: One concrete provisioning decision a chef can act on this week — ingredient-swap facts with hard numbers travel furthest in saves and shares.

posts.subhook: Off the heat, or the aroma leaves before the plate does
posts.caption: Whisky's esters and phenols boil off above roughly 78°C — add it to a simmering caramel and the aroma leaves the pan before the plate leaves the galley. So it goes in last, off the heat: 30 g of dark whisky whisked into the finished sauce with 3 g of flaky salt. The pour has its own physics. At 70–80°C the sauce wicks 4–5 mm into warm, skewered crumb inside a minute; poured cold, it gels and sits on top like a glaze nobody asked for. The service sequence: skewer the warm pudding 30-plus times, pour over a third of the sauce, give it 60 seconds to vanish, then cut and drench each plate with about 40 g more. The reel is one static overhead in real time — the surface going dark and glossy as it drinks. Keep the rest of the sauce hot in a jug for the second pour at the table; nobody has ever refused it. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Whisky's esters and phenols boil off above roughly 78°C, so it is whisked in off-heat — and the sauce must hit the warm, skewered sponge at 70–80°C to wick 4–5 mm into the crumb within a minute.
posts.brief: B&W overhead, one static take: a jug pours hot sauce onto the skewered slab and the surface turns glossy-dark in real time as it drinks.
posts.why: The pour is the dish's money shot — visible motion earns the reel format, and the 78°C volatility rule gives the spectacle real substance.

posts.hook: Raw garlic is a ticking clock
posts.subhook: One bulb, five chemically different ingredients
posts.caption: Crush a clove and you start a clock. The enzyme alliinase converts alliin into allicin — the pungent, eye-watering compound that defines raw garlic — and allicin degrades within hours. Apply heat first and the enzyme is destroyed, so allicin never forms at all. That single reaction is why one bulb behaves as five different ingredients. Raw: sharp, antimicrobial, unstable. Roasted: the Maillard reaction builds hundreds of sweet, caramelised compounds — squeeze it from the skin and it spreads. Confit at 80–100°C: the sulfur mellows into background flavour and the oil becomes a tool in its own right. Black garlic, fermented at 60–70°C for 4–6 weeks: allicin gone, replaced by S-allyl cysteine, a stable antioxidant, with melanoidins — the same browning compounds as aged balsamic — carrying the flavour. Powder: alliin without the enzyme, so it dissolves into garlic flavour with none of the raw bite; better in rubs and marinades than its reputation suggests. A chef who treats garlic as one thing is using 20% of its potential. Stock all five. Use each deliberately. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Crushing a clove triggers alliinase to create allicin, but heat destroys the enzyme first — so raw, roasted, confit, black and powdered garlic are five chemically different ingredients.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: one bulb exploded into five labelled states — raw clove, roasted head, confit in oil, black-garlic clove, powder mound — graphite on cool paper.
posts.why: The article's core science lesson split across five save-worthy slides — one form per slide, screenshot material for the station.

posts.subhook: Garlic and truffle share the same molecule
posts.caption: Garlic and truffle share dimethyl sulfide — the same molecule. Which means garlic cream finished with truffle oil is not layering flavour; it is stacking one compound twice and calling it depth. The sulfur chemistry that dominates garlic — allicin, diallyl disulfide, diallyl trisulfide — explains most of its pairings, including the surprising ones. Diallyl disulfide is why the whole allium family speaks the same language: onion, shallot, leek, chive. Methyl mercaptan is the literal bridge between garlic and washed-rind cheese — aged Camembert, Limburger, even durian. And when you roast garlic, pyrazines appear — the same compounds found in coffee, bread crust and roasted nuts, which is why a roasted-garlic and espresso rub converges instead of clashing. The lesson for the galley: read pairings at the molecular level before you build the plate. Some combinations that sound wrong are chemically inevitable — black garlic against dark chocolate, both loaded with melanoidins. And some that sound luxurious are just the same note played twice. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Garlic and truffle share dimethyl sulfide, so garlic cream plus truffle oil is molecular redundancy — the same compound stacked twice, not added complexity.
posts.brief: High-contrast B&W macro: a halved garlic bulb beside a whole black truffle on matte grey, a pencil-drawn line linking the two with a shared-molecule label.
posts.why: A contrarian pairing truth fine-dining cooks will argue about in the comments — high share and tag-a-chef potential.

posts.hook: Ninety degrees, ninety minutes, two tools
posts.subhook: The confit batch every rotation deserves
posts.caption: Peel two heads of garlic, cover the cloves with olive oil, and hold them at 90°C for 90 minutes. Strain. You now have two tools from one pan: soft confit cloves ready for instant use, and a garlic-infused oil for finishing. Refrigerate both and use within 2 weeks — then make a fresh batch every rotation. The chemistry does the mellowing for you. At 80–100°C the allicin pathway is suppressed, the harsh sulfur compounds soften into gentle background flavour, and the oil absorbs garlic-derived terpenes as it cooks. What comes out bears no resemblance to a raw clove: spreadable, quiet, and stable enough to plan a week of service around. Garlic earns its place on a boat for the same reason — whole bulbs keep 3–6 months in cool, dark, dry storage, longer than any other fresh ingredient in the larder. One provisioning stop covers months, and 90 minutes of low oven time turns the surplus into standing mise en place. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Two peeled heads covered in olive oil at 90°C for 90 minutes yield ready-to-use confit cloves plus a finishing oil — refrigerated, both hold for 2 weeks.
posts.brief: B&W overhead reel frame: peeled cloves submerged in oil in a small pan, thermometer reading 90°C, strained amber oil pouring into a jar beside a spread clove on toast.
posts.why: A visible-motion technique (peel, pour, barely-bubbling oil, strain, spread) with exact numbers — reels reach non-followers and the method drives saves.

mushrooms-the-new-centre.title: Mushrooms: The New Centre of the Plate

posts.subhook: A dried cap carries more glutamate than aged cheese
posts.caption: Dried shiitakes carry up to 1,060 mg of free glutamate per 100g — more than aged Parmesan. That is why a mushroom reads as meat: it is meaty in the biochemical sense, dense with the same savoury compounds that give hung beef and hard cheese their depth. The useful part for the galley is the synergy. Combine a glutamate source (shiitake, Parmesan, tomato, soy) with a nucleotide source (dried shiitake carries guanylate, kombu carries glutamate) and the perceived intensity multiplies rather than adds. Shiitake plus kombu dashi is the most potent example in any kitchen — glutamate meeting guanylate. It is also why mushroom risotto finished with Parmesan lands as one of the most umami-dense plates you can build, with no MSG involved. One caution from the piece: strong vinegars and heavy citrus flatten umami, so hold the acid back when mushrooms are the point. Stack your savoury sources instead of chasing brightness, and the flavour reads deeper for it. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Dried shiitakes carry up to 1,060 mg of free glutamate per 100g — more than Parmesan — and pairing them with a second glutamate or nucleotide source such as kombu dashi multiplies perceived umami rather than adding it.
posts.brief: High-contrast B&W macro: a cracked donko shiitake cap beside a shard of aged Parmesan on dark slate, raking side light picking out both textures.
posts.why: A concrete number that beats an ingredient everyone respects — saves and stitches into any pairing conversation.

posts.hook: The escalope that was never meat
posts.subhook: King oyster, scored and seared like a scallop
posts.caption: Slice a king oyster into 3cm paves, score a crosshatch into each cut face, and sear it hard in smoking-hot oil. The dense stem behaves like a scallop — it caramelises, holds its shape, and takes a glaze. Rest it, finish with soy and mirin, and send it as the main course. This is the mushroom that earns the centre of the plate, the place veal or a seared escalope held ten years ago. It is not a vegetarian footnote printed in smaller font; it is a protein with its own texture. King oyster is also the most forgiving fungus to provision: firm, dry, white stems that travel and store better than any other mushroom, so buy the ones with small caps and no yellowing. The move for the pass — slice thick, sear hard, spoon over XO sauce, so the scallop-like bite meets the most savoury condiment in Cantonese cooking. Guests who tell you they do not eat vegetarian tend to ask for seconds. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A king oyster sliced into 3cm paves, crosshatch-scored, seared hard and glazed with soy-mirin behaves like a scallop and holds the centre of the plate as a protein.
posts.brief: B&W action still: a thick king-oyster pave with a caramelised crosshatch face searing in a smoking pan, oil sheen and steam caught in hard directional light.
posts.why: A technique with visible motion — scoring, the hard sear, the glaze — that translates directly to a short reel and shows the centre-plate payoff.

posts.subhook: The 200g bag that outperforms your fridge
posts.caption: A 200g bag of dried porcini weighs almost nothing, takes no fridge space, and keeps indefinitely — and it will carry a month of stock, risotto and sauce base. On a boat where every litre of storage is contested, that ratio is the whole argument for dried mushrooms over fresh. Carry two profiles: dried shiitake for anything Asian, dried porcini for anything European, and both together for a stock that crosses cuisines. The soaking liquid is not waste — strain it through muslin and treat it as the most useful stock in the pantry. Blend dried porcini to a fine powder, keep it in an airtight jar, and a spoonful adds savoury depth to any rub, seasoning mix or sauce; it never expires. For the fresh mushrooms you do carry, one rule holds — paper bags, never plastic. Plastic traps moisture, they sweat, and they rot within days. King oysters last longest; enoki and maitake are fragile, so use them within 2-3 days. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A 200g bag of dried porcini weighs almost nothing and keeps indefinitely, yielding a month of stock, risotto and sauce base, while fresh mushrooms must be stored in paper — never plastic — or they rot within days.
posts.brief: B&W top-down flat-lay on a galley worktop: an open bag of dried porcini, a jar of mushroom powder, and a folded paper bag, hard single-source shadow.
posts.why: Pure yacht-galley logistics — the storage-per-gram maths and the no-plastic rule are the most saveable, uniquely on-brand takeaways in the piece.

posts.hook: The amino acid that fakes glutamate
posts.subhook: Why matcha reads as umami, not bitterness
posts.caption: Matcha broth tastes savoury, not bitter, and the reason is a single amino acid. L-theanine — found almost nowhere in nature outside Camellia sinensis and a few mushrooms — is structurally similar to glutamate, the molecule behind umami. Your palate reads a strong matcha the way it reads dashi: as depth, not astringency. That makes tea a legitimate umami source in savoury cooking, not a novelty. The applications are immediate. Whisk culinary-grade matcha into a dashi for a broth with more depth than either alone. Grind it into salt for a seared scallop. Steep it into warm cream for a ganache that holds bitterness, sweetness and savour in one line — Japanese confectioners have built on matcha's bitter-sweet contrast since the 14th century. Buy culinary grade, not ceremonial: it is cheaper, more robust, and made for heat. Bright green means fresh; dull olive means old — store it sealed, chilled, away from light. And remember every tea, green to black to pu-erh, is one species; processing, not botany, decides what it becomes. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: L-theanine, an amino acid essentially unique to tea, is structurally similar to glutamate — which is why matcha functions as an umami source in savoury cooking rather than reading as bitter.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: a chasen whisk mid-motion in a matcha bowl, with the L-theanine and glutamate skeletal structures sketched side by side in graphite above it.
posts.why: A counterintuitive molecular fact about a familiar pantry item — stops the scroll and repositions matcha for savoury work.

posts.subhook: Lapsang souchong, two minutes, no alcohol
posts.caption: Two minutes. That is the entire brewing window for lapsang souchong when you cook with it — any longer and the smoked black tea releases bitter tar compounds instead of clean smoke. Brewed short and reduced hard, it puts guaiacol and syringol into a jus: the same phenols found in smoked meats, charred wood and peated whisky. The result is smoky depth with no smoker, no liquid smoke and no alcohol — which matters on a boat with no deck space for smoking gear and charters that run dry. Swap it for the wine in a red-meat jus; it is most convincing under lamb and venison. For a brine, treat it like any tannin-rich black tea: tannins bind to proteins, firming texture and deepening colour the way a wine braise does. None of this is a stunt. Chinese kitchens have smoked duck over tea leaves, camphor wood and rice for centuries — the technique is older than most of the fonds we call classical. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Lapsang souchong brewed for exactly 2 minutes and reduced gives a jus real smoke — guaiacol and syringol, the phenols of smoked meat — without a smoker or alcohol; steep it longer and it turns to bitter tar.
posts.brief: High-grain B&W macro: smoke curling off loose lapsang leaves in a steel spoon; reel cover alternative — a dark jus glossing a lamb rack, spoon mid-nappe.
posts.why: Solves two real galley constraints at once (no smoker on board, alcohol-free charters) with visible motion — brew, strain, reduce, nappe — built for saves.

posts.hook: Five teas, fifty dollars, one season
posts.subhook: The whole tea kit a galley needs
posts.caption: Five loose-leaf teas cover an entire charter season of sauces, brines and infusions for under $50: hojicha for roast and nut, lapsang souchong for smoke, culinary matcha for colour and umami, jasmine pearls for floral, and one good Assam or Darjeeling for braising liquids and brines. Sealed airtight and kept from light they hold for months, at zero weight and almost zero locker space — gram for gram, the most efficient flavour on board. Three techniques do most of the work. Tea stock: brew double strength, strain, and use as the liquid under a braise or a mushroom risotto. Tea powder: grind any dry leaf in a spice grinder for rubs, shortbread, or a hojicha dusting on crème brûlée before the torch. Cold infusion: steep leaves overnight in cream or oil in the fridge — fat pulls terpenes and lipid-soluble volatiles that hot water misses. One rule is absolute: loose leaf only. Bags hold dust and fannings that dump bitter tannins fast and carry a fraction of the aromatics. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Five loose-leaf teas — hojicha, lapsang, culinary matcha, jasmine pearls and one good Assam — cover a full season of sauces, brines and infusions for under $50, at zero weight and months of sealed shelf life.
posts.brief: B&W flat-lay on brushed galley steel: five small mounds of loose leaf in a row, each labelled in pencil handwriting (hojicha, lapsang, matcha, jasmine, assam), hard side light.
posts.why: A concrete provisioning checklist (5 teas + 3 techniques = 8 natural slides) that chefs save and act on before the next shore run.

posts.subhook: The fix is on the stovetop, not in the oven.
posts.caption: A crumble floods because the apples go into a hot oven raw and run straight from crisp to mush with no stop in between. The fix happens on the stovetop, before the oven is involved. Melt 36 g of butter in a wide pan, add your apples and their liquid, and cook for about 8 minutes until the centre reads 71°C on a probe and the liquid has reduced to a glossy syrup rather than running water. That window matters: held between 50 and 80°C, an enzyme in the apple's cell walls, pectin methylesterase, strips methyl groups off the pectin and frees sites that cross-link with calcium into a firm, heat-stable lattice. The fruit then bakes fully tender without collapsing. Skip the step and no amount of oven time saves it. On a moving boat this is also why the filling never runs under swell once the dish is assembled. Two things to hold onto: a probe, and the patience to reduce to syrup, not water. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Holding cut apples at 71°C on the stovetop activates pectin methylesterase, which cross-links pectin with cell-wall calcium so the fruit bakes tender without collapsing.
posts.brief: Overhead B&W reel of the pre-cook: raw apple chunks hitting foaming butter, stirred as the liquid reduces to a glossy syrup, ending on a probe thermometer reading 71°C — tight and fast.
posts.why: The signature science of the piece — the 71°C move is the ownable, save-worthy fact that separates this crumble from every other recipe.

posts.hook: One apple is a broken crumble
posts.subhook: Firm for the edge, soft for the bind — 60:40 by weight.
posts.caption: Use one variety of apple and a crumble fails one of two ways: an all-firm apple stays in raw cubes, an all-soft one collapses to sauce. The dish wants both at once, so use two in a 60:40 split by weight — 720 g of a holds-shape apple to 480 g of one that melts. Firm, high-acid apples like Braeburn, Granny Smith or Pink Lady keep their edges because their cell walls are tougher and their pectin breaks down more slowly. Soft, low-acid ones like Golden Delicious, McIntosh or Cortland break down to a sauce that binds the whole spoonful together. Cut both to 2 cm chunks — small enough that the firm apple cooks through, large enough that the soft one does not vanish. You get chunk and bind in a single spoonful, the texture the memory expects from a proper crumble. It costs nothing extra and it is the difference most home versions miss. Keep the ratio and you can build it from whatever two apples the provisioner sends aboard. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: A 60:40 blend of a firm high-acid apple and a soft low-acid one gives both chunk and bind, where a single variety turns all-raw or all-mush.
posts.brief: Two apples side by side on a marble board in B&W, one whole and firm, one halved and slumping, a knife laid between them — the 60:40 split made visible.
posts.why: A concrete, do-it-tonight ingredient rule; the 60:40 split is easy to remember and instantly shareable to a chef mid-provisioning.

posts.subhook: The common 180°C is 25 degrees too cool.
posts.caption: Most crumbles bake pale and soggy on top for one reason: the oven is set 25 degrees too cool. The common instruction is 180°C, and it is wrong. Bake at 200°C (400°F) instead. The higher heat crisps the topping properly and keeps the bake short — 35 to 45 minutes — so the filling does not over-reduce to jam while it waits. Scatter the topping loose and craggy over fruit you have first cooled to warm, and never pack it down, because the air gaps are what let it crisp. Bake on a tray, rotating once, until the top is deep golden with mahogany edges and thick syrup bubbles through the cracks; the filling should read 93 to 96°C. Then the part everyone rushes: rest it 10 minutes. The filling has to fall from about 95°C to 75°C for the cornflour to set, and cut too soon the juices pour instead of spooning. A hotter oven, then a short wait — that is the whole route to a top that shatters. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Baking a crumble at 200°C rather than the common 180°C is what crisps the top and keeps the filling from cooking down to jam.
posts.brief: High-contrast B&W close-up of a baked crumble's craggy top, mahogany edges and thick syrup bubbling through a crack, faint steam rising off the surface.
posts.why: A crisp myth-bust against the standard recipe temperature — corrective, contrarian, and quick to act on.

posts.subhook: Why the bird and the broth must never share a pot
posts.caption: Muscle proteins squeeze out their water above 65–70°C and contract hard past 75°C, so chicken simmered in its own stock for 3 hours turns to leathery thread. The bones want the opposite treatment: collagen only converts to gelatin between 70°C and 90°C, and it needs those 3 hours to give up its body. One pot cannot do both jobs — which is why almost every home version arrives grey and tired. The perfect version splits the build. Roast 2 kg of carcasses and wings at 220°C to deep mahogany, cover with 4 L of cold water, and hold a bare simmer at 85–90°C for 3 hours, bubbles breaking only occasionally. Poach 800 g of bone-in thighs apart in salted water, 18–22 minutes to 74°C internal (breast to 63°C), then pull into hand-sized pieces. The two tracks meet at the bowl: vegetables sweated in schmaltz, pulled chicken folded in for the final 2 minutes only, noodles cooked separately and ladled over. Broth with body, meat still juicy, both arriving clear. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Chicken muscle turns to leathery thread past 75°C while bones need 3 hours at 85–90°C to convert collagen to gelatin, so the stock and the meat must be cooked in separate pots and combined only at the bowl.
posts.brief: B&W overhead of two pots on one stove — tall stockpot steaming, low poaching pan still — hard side light; alt: technical-pencil plate of the split build with the two temperature tracks (85–90°C stock / 74°C meat) annotated.
posts.why: The article's core thesis as a save-worthy step carousel (principle → stock track → meat track → assembly) — long weekend dwell time suits multi-slide technique content.

posts.subhook: 300 g of chicken feet double the gelatin of a 4-litre stock
posts.caption: Chicken feet are roughly 70% collagen by dry weight — the highest connective-tissue ratio of any part of the bird — and 300 g of them will double the gelatin of a 4 L stock without changing its flavour. That is the difference between a broth that clings to the spoon and one that runs off it like water. The ratio is simple: bones 100%, feet 15%, water 200% — about 2 kg of roasted carcasses to 4 L of cold water yields 2.5 L of stock that sets to jelly when chilled. Prep takes 2 minutes: drop the feet into rolling water for 60 seconds, shock in ice, peel the yellow membrane in sheets, trim the nails, and add them to the pot raw. The shtetl cooks of Eastern Europe built this dish on an old laying hen because connective tissue was what they had; the feet are the same thrift doing the same heavy lifting. The jelly test never lies. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Chicken feet are roughly 70% collagen by dry weight, so 300 g added to a 4 L stock doubles its gelatin without changing the flavour.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate of a single chicken foot drawn exploded-diagram style with a collagen callout on cool paper; alt: B&W macro of chilled stock set to jelly holding a spoon upright.
posts.why: One surprising ingredient fact with a visible pass/fail test (the jelly) — high save and share value among working chefs scrolling between services.

posts.subhook: Fish sauce in chicken soup — and no one will ever know
posts.caption: Fish sauce carries up to 1,300 mg of free glutamate per 100 g, and a Parmigiano rind around 1,200 mg — and at the right dose, both read as savoury depth in a chicken soup, never as themselves. The build: 30 g of parmesan rind goes into the stock for the full 3-hour simmer; 15 g of fish sauce goes in off the heat at the very end, alongside 20 g of lemon juice — citrus volatiles degrade with heat, so the acid is always last. Nobody at the table will taste fish or cheese. They will taste a chicken soup that seems to have simmered a day longer than it did, rounder and more complete, and they will not be able to say why. That is the standard for seasoning at this level: the guest tastes the dish, not the trick. And when a broth still reads flat, the order of operations is fixed — salt first, umami anchor second, lemon last, always off the heat. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Fish sauce (up to 1,300 mg free glutamate per 100 g) and parmesan rind (~1,200 mg) at 15 g and 30 g read as invisible savoury depth in chicken soup, with 20 g of lemon added off-heat last because citrus volatiles degrade with heat.
posts.brief: B&W still life: small fish-sauce bottle and a parmesan rind beside a clear bowl of broth, hard raking light; pencil-plate alt: the two glutamate values drawn as a graphite bar comparison.
posts.why: Secret-ingredient angle backed by hard glutamate numbers — natural comment driver (chefs will confess their own invisible anchors) with no clickbait needed.

posts.subhook: The water leaves before the flour ever arrives.
posts.caption: Roughly a quarter of a banana's weight leaves as water when you roast it — and that water is the difference between a deep, jammy loaf and a gummy one. Lay 900 g of black-spotted bananas on a tray whole, skins on, at 150°C (300°F) for 25–30 minutes, until the skins are fully black and the flesh is jammy. You end with about 680 g of concentrated, caramelised fruit; pour off any liquid that pools, because that puddle is pure dilution. Raw mashed banana does the opposite — it carries its water straight into the flour, which is exactly where the wet band near the base of so many loaves comes from. It also earns its keep on charter: roast in bulk on a provisioning day, portion the mash into 340 g bags and freeze flat for up to 3 months, and the loaf becomes a 25-minute job any morning after. In the full Blueprint the roasted fruit runs at 142% of the flour's weight — the banana outweighs the flour, which is why concentrating it matters more than anything you could add. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Roasting bananas whole at 150°C for 25–30 minutes drives off roughly a quarter of their weight as water while caramelising the sugars, preventing the gummy crumb that raw mashed banana causes.
posts.brief: B&W overhead photo: whole roasted bananas on a lined tray, skins split and fully blackened, jammy flesh escaping at the seams — hard side light, deep shadow.
posts.why: Fixes the most common banana-bread failure (gummy crumb) on the day people actually bake — high save-and-share utility.

posts.subhook: Bicarbonate is the whole engine of a quick bread.
posts.caption: Bicarbonate of soda does two jobs in a banana loaf, and both decide the result. First it neutralises the ripe banana's acid — a quick bread rises on that chemistry, not on yeast. Then, with the acid spent, the leftover alkalinity raises the batter's pH and accelerates the Maillard browning that gives the crust its nutty, caramel-edged colour. The dose is a knife edge: 12 g against 480 g of flour, about 2.5% in baker's terms. Too little and the loaf bakes pale and faintly sour; too much and unreacted alkalinity tastes soapy and metallic. Weigh it. The soda also starts releasing gas the moment it meets the fruit's acid, so the batter goes into the tin promptly — and it is why you never freeze raw batter: the lift dies in the freezer, so bake first, freeze after. The recipe was built around this reaction in the 1930s, when soda became a cheap, shelf-stable grocery and a blackened banana was too valuable to bin. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Bicarbonate of soda both neutralises the banana's acid and raises the batter's pH to accelerate Maillard browning — at 12 g per 480 g flour, too little bakes pale and sour, too much tastes soapy and metallic.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate: cross-sectioned loaf with the crust hatched dark, annotated acid-plus-soda reaction arrows, and a pale-vs-browned crust comparison in graphite.
posts.why: Chemistry explainer with a built-in troubleshooting payoff (pale-sour vs soapy-metallic) — positions the science authority and drives saves.

posts.subhook: Sixty grams of umami that reads as sweetness.
posts.caption: White miso in a sweet batter does not read as savoury — it reads as more. Shiro miso carries free glutamates, and in banana bread that umami amplifies perceived sweetness and depth instead of announcing itself; at 60 g against 480 g of flour the miso is undetectable as miso. It has to be white, though — red is fermented harder and its salt and sharpness push through the loaf. The trick rides on the batter's other browning investment: 230 g of butter cooked over medium heat for 6–8 minutes until the milk solids turn golden and smell of toasted hazelnut, then the 300 g light brown sugar, 100 g caster sugar and the miso whisked in while the butter is still warm, so the paste dissolves smooth instead of streaking. If you want the echo at service, the same pairing becomes a glaze — 2 tbsp brown butter, 1 tbsp miso, icing sugar to a drizzle — and a slice brûléed with a thread of sugar turns a crew loaf into a guest plate. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: 60 g of white (shiro) miso whisked into the warm brown butter and sugars amplifies perceived sweetness through free glutamates while staying completely undetectable as miso.
posts.brief: B&W macro photo: a whisk pulling a ribbon of miso through glossy brown butter, toasted milk-solid flecks sharp in the highlights against a dark rim of pan.
posts.why: Counterintuitive pantry trick — the contrarian claim earns comments and shares from working chefs on a quick midday scroll.

port-call-venice.title: Port Call Venice: Provisioning a City With No Roads

posts.hook: The van stops at Piazzale Roma
posts.subhook: Why every Venice delivery ends on a boat
posts.caption: No road vehicle can reach a yacht berthed in Venice. The van you loaded with a fortnight's provisions stops at Piazzale Roma — the last point on the map a wheel can touch — and everything beyond it moves by boat, across a city of 118 islands and 417 bridges. So the bulk run follows a fixed pattern. Rent a van at Mestre rail station. Do the supermarket sweep at Esselunga and Ipercoop Nave de Vero, about 10 minutes from Piazzale Roma. Pre-order the serious cuts — wagyu, dry-aged, whole animals — from Macelleria Carpitella 48–72 hours ahead; they prepare specialty cuts to order, not for the floor. Then drive to Piazzale Roma, where the marina's portage boat takes over for the final leg to the quay. Budget €100–200 for that transfer, and it is still the cheapest reliable way to fill a superyacht galley here. One more discipline: brief your yacht agent in writing 48 hours before arrival — two weeks if you are coming during Biennale, when everything that floats gets booked first. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: In Venice no delivery vehicle can pass Piazzale Roma, so the bulk provisioning run is a rented van in Mestre plus a €100–200 marina portage boat for the final leg.
posts.brief: B&W photo: provision crates stacked on a portage boat at the Piazzale Roma quay, mooring lines and cardboard in hard morning side-light.
posts.why: The counterintuitive logistics of a roadless city is the article's strongest share trigger, and the 4-step Mestre run carries a genuine slide sequence.

posts.hook: Never print moeche on a menu
posts.subhook: The lagoon's soft-shell crab obeys the boats, not the chef
posts.caption: The 2025 spring moeche harvest effectively failed. Moeche — the Venetian lagoon's soft-shell crabs, caught in the brief moult and fried whole — appear in just two windows a year, April to May and October to November, and the harvest is now fragile enough that climate shifts and the invasive blue crab can erase a season. Which is why working chefs in Venice follow one rule: never put moeche on a printed menu. Offer them as a verbal special on the day you actually hold them, and keep a substitute ready — cuttlefish with its ink kept aside, or schie. The place to settle it is the Rialto Pescheria, trading on the same spot since 1097, open Tuesday to Saturday, 07:30–13:00, closed Sunday and Monday. Ask the moecanti directly what came off the boats that morning; their answer, not the menu plan, decides the dish. It is the sharpest version of a rule that travels to every port: the more fragile the ingredient, the later you commit it to paper. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: Moeche have two short windows a year and a harvest fragile enough that the 2025 spring effectively failed — so they are sold as a verbal special, never on a printed menu.
posts.brief: Technical-pencil plate of a moeca (soft-shell crab), dorsal view with moult-stage callouts, graphite on cool paper.
posts.why: Insider seasonal-scarcity knowledge that saves a chef from a printed-menu failure — high save value for working crew.

posts.subhook: The carrello rule every Venetian nonna already knows
posts.caption: A metered Venetian water taxi starts at €15 and adds roughly €2 per minute, plus a €5 call charge and a €15 surcharge after 22:00. A vaporetto ticket costs €9.50, is valid 75 minutes, and carries your load free: ACTV's baggage allowance is three pieces per ticket — two within 120 cm of summed dimensions, one within 150 cm — which means a standard granny carrello, the loaded shopping trolley of every Venetian household, rides legally. Line 2 runs the Grand Canal from San Marco to Rialto in about 15 minutes, so a dawn market haul moves across the city for less than a crew coffee round. The catch is timing: a full carrello has no place in the commuter rush, so work before 08:00 or after 19:00. Validate at the gate — fines start at €60 and there is no validation on board. Save the water taxi for the days a flat rate earns it: €150–250, negotiated before boarding, for a run loaded with crates. The full piece is on Littoralicious @littoralicious — link in bio.
posts.fact: ACTV's free baggage allowance — three pieces per €9.50 vaporetto ticket, carrello included — makes the off-peak vaporetto the cheapest way to move a market haul across Venice.
posts.brief: B&W photo: a loaded shopping carrello parked at a vaporetto boarding gate, wet deck, rope and rail textures, no faces.
posts.why: Concrete money-per-unit numbers crew can screenshot and apply on their next port call — pure utility drives saves and sends.

flavordb-compound-pairing.title: FlavorDB: The Cheat Code for Flavor Pairing

posts.subhook: The open database behind modern food pairing.
posts.caption: Every dish that ever worked worked because two ingredients shared volatile compounds: molecules that reach your olfactory receptors at the same moment and read as harmony.
